---
title: "Small-mass limit of Wigner angles"
engine: julia
format:
  gfm: default
---

PR [KaiHabermann/decayangle#85](https://github.com/KaiHabermann/decayangle/pull/85)
tries to treat an exactly massless final-state particle by replacing its rest
frame with an auxiliary frame. That is useful for testing, but the exact
massless rest frame does not exist.

Here we ask a nearby, well-defined question: keep the usual massive
construction, make one final-state mass small, and watch the matching Wigner
rotation.

```{julia}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false
#| output: false
using InstructionalDecayTrees
using FourVectors
ENV["GKSwstype"] = "100"
using LaTeXStrings
using Plots

const IDT = InstructionalDecayTrees
theme(:boxed, fontfamily = "Computer Modern")
wrap2(a) = mod(a + π, 2π) - π
```

## Four-body example

Compare the paths to particle 1 for `((12)3)4` and `(13)(24)`.

```{julia}
q1(m) = FourVector(0.20, -0.10, -0.50; M = m)
q2 = FourVector(0.35, 0.22, 0.28; M = 0.45)
q3 = FourVector(-0.18, 0.31, -0.12; M = 0.60)
q4 = FourVector(-0.37, -0.43, 0.34; M = 0.80)

path_123_12_1 = (
    ToHelicityFrame((1, 2, 3)),
    ToHelicityFrame((1, 2)),
    ToHelicityFrame(1),
)
path_13_1 = (ToHelicityFrame((1, 3)), ToHelicityFrame(1));
```

The relative transform is a rotation. Close to the massless limit the full
Lorentz-factor decode becomes numerically delicate, so we read the rotation
from the tracked SU(2) matrix.

```{julia}
masses = 10.0 .^ range(0, -3; length = 20)
scan = []

for m in masses
    cmp = compare_instruction_paths(path_123_12_1, path_13_1, (q1(m), q2, q3, q4))
    w = wigner_zyz(cmp.relative)
    push!(scan, (m = m, θ = w.θ, phase = wrap2(w.ϕ + w.ψ)))
end

last(scan)
```

The non-collinear angle goes to zero:

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
mkpath(joinpath(@__DIR__, "src", "assets"))

logm = log10.([row.m for row in scan])
mass_ticks = ([-3, -2, -1, 0], [L"10^{-3}", L"10^{-2}", L"10^{-1}", L"10^{0}"])
theta_over_pi = [row.θ / π for row in scan]

pθ = plot(
    logm,
    theta_over_pi;
    xlabel = L"m_1",
    ylabel = L"\theta / \pi",
    xticks = mass_ticks,
    xlims = (-3.05, 0.05),
    label = false,
    marker = :circle,
)
savefig(pθ, joinpath(@__DIR__, "src", "assets", "four-body-wigner-theta-boxed.svg"))
```

![Four-body small-mass Wigner theta](assets/four-body-wigner-theta-boxed.svg)

At the limit, the Wigner rotation is a helicity phase. For a diagonal
spin-rotation matrix,

```math
D_{\lambda',\lambda}(\phi,\theta,\gamma)
\xrightarrow[\theta\to 0]{}
\delta_{\lambda',\lambda}\,\exp\!\left[-i\lambda(\phi+\gamma)\right].
```

So the useful quantity is the phase angle `ϕ + γ`, not the two Euler angles
separately.

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
phase_over_pi = [row.phase / π for row in scan]

pγ = plot(
    logm,
    phase_over_pi;
    xlabel = L"m_1",
    ylabel = L"(\phi + \gamma) / \pi",
    xticks = mass_ticks,
    xlims = (-3.05, 0.05),
    label = false,
    marker = :circle,
)
savefig(pγ, joinpath(@__DIR__, "src", "assets", "four-body-wigner-phase-boxed.svg"))
```

![Four-body small-mass Wigner phase](assets/four-body-wigner-phase-boxed.svg)

The limiting phase is fractional:

```{julia}
last(scan).phase / π
```

For this `((12)3)4` versus `(13)(24)` comparison it is about `0.7685π`, so
the matching angle is not pinned to a special integer multiple of `π`.

## Takeaway

- An exactly massless particle has no rest frame, so the massive helicity
  construction cannot simply be evaluated at `m = 0`.
- The small-mass limit is regular in the sense that `θ → 0`.
- At the limit, only the little-group phase `ϕ + γ` is meaningful.

## Possible follow-up

There is a puzzle in the simpler three-body case. For the points checked so
far, the limiting phase is somewhat trivial: either `0` / `2π`, or `π`.

This is not yet evidence for a general rule. A useful next check would be to
generate phase-space points and test whether the three-body limiting phase is
always one of those special values, or whether it changes point by point.
